content:
Comparative performance under accelerated aging reveals a divergent ranking of encapsulant adhesion stability depending on whether the test is conducted at coupon level or at module level under damp heat conditions. POE encapsulant coupons demonstrated the best adhesion stability under thermal aging, followed by EPE then EVA [REF:9]. This coupon-level hierarchy positions polyolefin elastomers as the most thermally durable option among the three chemistries tested, with EVA ranking last in thermal adhesion retention [REF:9].

At the module level under damp heat conditions, however, the ranking partially inverts. EVA laminates showed less peel force degradation after damp heat than POE samples [REF:10]. This discordance between coupon-level thermal aging and module-level damp heat performance indicates that moisture exposure introduces a distinct degradation pathway that penalizes POE more severely than dry thermal stress alone [REF:9][REF:10]. The difference may relate to lamination processing: a higher lamination temperature was beneficial for peel force and gel content before and after aging for cross-linking encapsulants [REF:10], suggesting that EVA's crosslinked network -- when properly processed at elevated lamination temperature -- confers improved moisture resistance at the module level.

Both hot-dry and hot-humid aging protocols produce measurable adhesion loss across all three encapsulant chemistries. Adhesion energy Gc decreased for EVA, POE, and EPE encapsulants after 5,000 hours of both hot-dry (90 degC, ~1% RH) and hot-humid (90 degC, 60% RH) aging [REF:2]. This universal decline confirms that no single encapsulant chemistry among those tested is immune to prolonged thermal or hydrothermal stress, though the magnitude of Gc reduction varies by material and humidity condition [REF:2][REF:9].

| Metric | EVA | POE | EPE | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coupon adhesion stability (thermal aging) | Lowest | Best | Intermediate | [REF:9] |
| Module-level peel force retention (damp heat) | Less degradation | More degradation | Not reported | [REF:10] |
| Gc after 5,000 h hot-dry (90 degC, ~1% RH) | Decreased | Decreased | Decreased | [REF:2] |
| Gc after 5,000 h hot-humid (90 degC, 60% RH) | Decreased | Decreased | Decreased | [REF:2] |
| Benefit from higher lamination temperature | Yes (gel content, peel force) | Not reported | Not reported | [REF:10] |

On balance, the available evidence presents a nuanced picture: POE offers superior adhesion stability under dry thermal cycling at coupon scale [REF:9], yet EVA retains more peel force under damp heat at module scale [REF:10], and all three materials suffer significant Gc loss after 5,000 hours of either hot-dry or hot-humid exposure [REF:2]. The absence of DVS-PEI from these comparative studies represents a critical gap. Quantitative data on DVS-PEI adhesion energy under both hot-dry and hot-humid protocols, direct head-to-head comparisons with EVA and POE at matched aging durations, and assessment of failure mode transitions (interfacial versus cohesive) for DVS-PEI interfaces remain unaddressed in the current evidence base.

**Key Findings**

- POE coupons demonstrated the best adhesion stability under thermal aging, followed by EPE then EVA [REF:9].
- EVA laminates showed less peel force degradation after damp heat than POE at module level, with higher lamination temperature improving peel force and gel content for cross-linking encapsulants [REF:10].
- Adhesion energy Gc decreased for EVA, POE, and EPE after 5,000 hours of both hot-dry and hot-humid aging [REF:2].
- Coupon-level and module-level rankings diverge: POE leads under dry thermal stress [REF:9] but trails EVA under damp heat [REF:10].
- DVS-PEI encapsulants are absent from all available head-to-head comparisons, representing a priority evidence gap.
